top_profane.text:
It lasted for about a year. When it worked, it was totally fine. Plenty of sound, great low end. Then it stopped working. I took it to a local guy and he replaced the ground circuit connector (I might be wrong about that term). After he fixed it, It was several minutes into my next gig. I took it back to him and said it was a bad transformer and Behringer no longer made the part.

If you are a serious musician and need reliable equipment, do not bother with Behringer. It you want cheap toys that will frustrate you and ultimately fail, get some Behringer crap and don't push them hard. Every musician I know has had their Behringer stuff crap out on them at some point--knobs on mixers stop working, blown speakers, etc. You get what you pay for. For the record, I played on Peavey's that were 30 years old and they were absolute workhorses. I also gigged with the Yamaha Stagepas for--again, total work horse. Pay a little more a buy a reputable brand.... you'll save in the long run.

It actually INFURIATES ME that Behringer stole my money!!! I don't care if they are low priced.... THEY STOLE MY MONEY! And I had to pay another $100 to the local guy to attempt to fix it!!!!! Congrats Behringer---you have produced the world's most expensive non-working speaker!!!!!!!

Oh and for the record, I only used this craptastic speaker as a stage wedge
top_profane.title: If you hate yourself and your money, buy Behringer

top_profane.text:
I come from a family of musicians and have been playing guitar since I was 7 years old - 40+ years. My first guitar was a 60's era Stratocaster, and it was a great guitar to learn on.

Needless to say, I was really stoked when I met, and was offered a job as a Maintenance Mechanic/Electrician, by Leo Fender himself at their Corona plant back in the 80's. It turned into a big disappointment though. I remember, when his production manager gave me a tour of their plant, how old and worn-out their machines were. It appeared to me to be more of a third world factory rather than one of the largest and most popular guitar manufacturers in the world. It was apparent that over the years they did not put much back into the company when it came to maintenance and upkeep, let alone investing in new machinery.

I was willing to accept the job for considerably less than I was used to earning - just to have the opportunity to work for Fender. But negotiating wages with Leo Fender was like trying to squeeze blood out of a turnip. I simply couldn't accept the starvation wages that he was apparently used to paying his mostly immigrant production employees.

Anyway, I have pretty much stayed away from Fenders for the last 25 years. The last two guitars that I purchased, were a Jackson-Dinky and a Gibson-Les Paul Studio. I then seen this MIM Fender Stratocaster here o
top_profane.title: Disappointing!

Let me preface by saying I was skeptical about this instrument for some time. I haven't had the best luck with sub $200 guitars, most have been utter crap. So imagine my relief when upon inspection and a few weeks of playing, that this bass ain't no slouch.

I bought a really crappy amp to practice with, dang thing wouldn't even go past 3 on the volume knob, I was worried the bass pickups were too weak to produce a decent sound. I swapped amps with my crate guitar amp and recorded the ensuing jam with a friend. Talk about thick sound! Color me impressed.

Now don't get it confused, as others have said, this thing will require set up. I had to adjust the truss rod several full turns to get the slinkys I put on to get off the fret board, infact, i think I stripped it out, crappy chinese pot metal. So be aware of that. Luckily, the action was just about perfect before the strip lol.

I also had to tighten the knobs, as they were loose and about to fall off, no biggie. There are 2 volume, and 1 tone, and as others have said, the tone doesn't alter much, well, it appeared so on my crappy amp, but on my crate, the tone knob made a noticeable change. The volume knobs also shut off completely with an 8th of a turn, no range whatsoever. I am planning on throwing in some better pickups later, but as it stands, all in all, the sound will do.
top_profane.title: Cheap bass, quality sound

So I was bummed to find that they released something called the R24 just a month or so after I bought the R16, which added features like 8 more tracks and a drum machine/sampler that I ABSOLUTELY WANTED on the R16. However, I still want to review this, particularly as it can be had on sale for $350 or so while the R24 is $500, which IS a significant amount of money to amateur people trying to make music, which is who this is really for.

I looked for months to try and find something like the R16, something that seemed to have all the features I'd need to record my band's music and get them going. When I found it I was skeptical, because it seemed weird that there would be one device that could record with external mics AND internal mics, and allow you to mix, edit, and master the audio, AND even include built-in effects. I didn't believe that anything would be that layman-friendly. But the R16 really is.

After a day or so of reading the manual, I finally figured everything out. It takes a while to get the hang of using the wheel and knowing which menu holds what, but once you do, you can do SO MUCH with this little thing. So damn much it's amazing. You can add an infinite number of effects if you learn how to bounce the tracks; you can swap them so that you can fully utilize all 16 tracks; you can edit everything and EQ everything and pan and fade and then
top_profane.title: Best purchase of my life

top_profane.text:
A quality audio recording system is essential in laying down professional-grade tracks rather than the normal, mediocre, amateurish audio which is so prevalent amongst basement/indie recording artists, unfortunately. As someone who has worked in the music industry for many years, and now in the film and television industry, I've personally used or been exposed to some of the best (and worst) audio recording equipment available, and I've learned that the cost of the equipment is not necessarily the top consideration when selecting quality audio recording components, among which the microphone should be the first and most important component evaluated and selected of the entire mix.

So many amateurs who wish to set up their own home audio recording studios max out their credit lines to buy the top, name-brand, latest craze in mixers, stands, isolation accesories, speakers, computer equipment, etc., etc., but they often neglect to buy the best quality microphone, or the correct type of microphone, first and foremost. Strange, considering the first piece of equipment touched by the talent's voice or musical instrument's soundwaves is the microphone, right?

More often than not, these home-brew artists do not understand which microphone type to select for what their specific recording needs will be, or they figure they can skimp on the cost of the microphone its
top_profane.title: You will not be disappointed with this microphone!!! Superb quality!!!

Ok, so I am an elementary orchestra teacher.
We refer to these as “violin shaped objects” (VSOs). I bought this as a drunken joke because I wanted to see how crappy it is. I will say I am slightly disappointed that it wasn’t as crappy as I was hoping it would be. I don’t want to show it to my students because I don’t want them to go order blue violins on Amazon. I still am deciding if I should keep it as a walk decoration/something for my kids to sign, or as the “violin of shame” for kids that forget their instruments, or if I should return it because I have no real need for it. I can’t use it really as an example of what NOT to get, as I had originally hoped.
I will say: I’m impressed that the strings were decently wound on the correct pegs! And, they actually stayed in tune, and the fine tuners actually work.

However, the following are reasons you should NOT get this for your child:
1) the bridge is not cut right. AND, you can’t expect a beginner to know how to put it on correctly! There aren’t even grooves for the strings to go in. It’s impossible for a beginner to play one string at a time.
2) the bow is sh*t. Seriously. Impressive that it only lost one hair in my trial run...but still a POS.
3) the fingerboard is crooked. So is the tailpiece. Therefore the alignment of the strings is off, and therefore the intonation is incon
top_profane.title: “Don’t do it.” - every good violin teacher, ever

Wanted to like it, it is such a classic. I still have it on my pedal board, but it is coming off, even though I was just playing through it yesterday. Here are the problems:

1. Too Limited - It has only one sound in it, and you can only make it go faster or slower.

2. The high and low points of the phasing/frequencies affected are fixed, and they are fixed in terrible spots/frequencies. The Phase 90 always goes too low in frequency and then when it's down there it makes the low end super loud, it essentially blows up the low end, making the low end of the guitar stick out in the mix only for that time it approaches, hits and then ascends out of the low end of its cycle. When the phase is low, it makes the guitar step over everything else, bass, vocals, drums, keys, other guitar. I dread the phaser reaching the bottom of its cycle, I cringe in anticipation each time. You can't set your guitar volume low so you sit well in the mix in the low part of the Phase 90's cycle because you won't be heard for the rest... most of the Phase 90's cycle. Basically you'll be playing, and no one will hear you until the Phase 90 goes into the bottom of its cycle, then the guitar will be super bassey and whack and still conflict with all other elements of the band/mix, and then when the phaser cycles up, you'll disappear again.

3. There is only one depth of the p

Both guitars have never had a post issue, and I wrapped the second set 6th with protective tape on the off chance the string was delicate there (but why?), it's not the post, it's a defect in the wrapping, just like for the buyers who had the G string separate. I would pay the price if these strings were reliable, but I guess I'm done with my favorite string, because I won't put up with this crap!!!

AND the brand new G sting also sounds really off, so maybe it is going to fail.

Also these string ends when tuning scraped the crap out of the inside of the slot head on a pristine guitar! I've never had a string do that before.

If you get a set that actually works they are great, but it's a real crap shoot!

top_profane.text: First, you will have to bore this out YOURSELF once you get it to make it even remotely usable for more than raspy fart noises. It's bore is laughably small and unusable. Then, once you do bore it out yourself, you will spend time trying to smooth and edge the canal to get a clean sound. Don't worry, you won't. The pitch will be crap, the tone will be crap, the ability to even get a proper calling sound at all will be like trying to get a broadway musical by punching a goose repeatedly in the face. Hmmm...what could a novice/hobbyist possibly say about the Zoom F8 that might be of interest.

I do mostly product featurettes for work (really short videos), or record training classes for clients (really long, maybe two hours plus). Some fits and starts in podcasting, but really the little work stuff is the main thing.

When first starting, I was using an iPhone for these videos, and iMovie for “post”. The iPhone sucked because the video quality at the time was kinda meh (iphone 4), video quality was too dark or too bright without enough detail, and the audio noise floor and background noise was so loud that it wasn't always easy to hear people. Plus, the quality of the voices was very bad, and there was nothing you could do about it.

I upgraded to a DSLR camera, and that improved video so much I haven’t messed with it since, but sound improved only marginally. Then added the Rode VideoMic pro, which improved sound marginally again. To get a little more control, I did get a twin mic setup that recorded in the left and right tracks through a saramonic audio interface. Through it all, it was intensely frustrating because the sound quality never sounded the way I wanted.

Very quickly I discovered that for training videos and product featurettes, people were okay with amateurish video quality as long as the camera was very still, bu
top_rant.title: 8 tracks of XLR input for under $1K + pro features.  Hard to beat.

I’ve owned this and the older two-12" model for over five years now. They’ve been used almost daily for lessons, plus countless gigs, big and small. I can help you get the most from this versatile club amp.

First of all, the modeling: It's pretty good! The only tricky issue is Fender intentionally mis-labels some amp models to perpetuate the myth of their most famous amps. They call the good British sounds tweed, champ or blackface, while the British labeled amps are actually just stuper-gain versions of mondo metal madness. So, for reasonable adult players that means; use the models with Fender brand associations. You'll get better tone and distortions that don't suck.

Don’t worry! There are plenty of stupid sounds in here if you are a case of arrested development with little talent or taste. And of course, you can always add the latest pedal from your ridiculously large collection for special effects. Practical minded old pros tend to carry less and do more. Either way, this amp has you covered.

Effects: These have a good musical assortment of usable effects, plus some cheesy gimmick combinations in factory memory. Fender is obviously trying to tempt young players by including ridiculous radical effect combinations. Best practice is to avoid or overwrite them with usable patches.

Starting with aesthetics. This is an excellent looking board. It's also super light (although there are some tradeoffs, as we will see shortly). The Krome is jet black and there are no annoying red, blue, green or yellow lights competing for my attention, only subtle light purple lights that illuminate function and mode buttons. I don't use Ableton. There, I said it. I still have hardware synths and a sampler. I guess that I'm old-school insomuch that I really like the feel of keys, strings, and (oddly) pads that respond to touch. I sold a MPC1000 a few months ago as it was its features redundant in the setup, it didn't have the ease of use or some of the things that I expected from such an expensive instrument. I've been wanting drum pads that were flexible enough to control some software synth stuff *and* be programmable so it could manage some of the hardware, too. The Maschine that seemed suitable was 5 bills, which was way too much, imho, for the use that it would get - and the smaller "mini" controllers seemed way too simple and cheaply made. When this was released it was originally like 350 or 400 - again, too much for what use it was to receive in my setup. When the price dropped, it was enough of a motivator for a try.

So, here's the down and dirty. The fit and finish are a cut above what was expected for the price. The 100 dollar controllers on the market were always a turn-off because they seemed that they were worth like 20. Cheap and plastic-eeey. This isn't the most solid gear around - it isn't precision cut or extruded aluminum, but it is well made enough that it doesn't creak in my hands - it actually feels sturdy. Out of the box, it was clean and unscuff

top_exclaim.text:
I have been buying, playing and selling guitars for about 30 years. I bought this case from a picture and the ad said that it would house "most" dreadnought type guitar. I bought the case for my Epiphone AJ series which has an unusually long headstock. No matter how much I pushed, shoved, jammed and pryed, my Epiphone was not going to fit in the case that I received swiftly and problem free. I was heart broken.

The case itself cannot be beat for the price, or even a much higher price for that matter. As it turned out, I had a case identical to the Stagg that housed my hand made Schecter Diamond series acoustic years back. My Schecter was valued at $2200.00 and I felt secure that my guitar was secure. That was before Schecter turned cheap Chinese and started making junk.

I ultimately bought a standard acoustic guitar for my daughter- primarily to make some use of the case that I could not use myself.

Perhaps after reading this Amazon will take note of my loss of expectation and send me some coupons for future musical item purchases.

HEY -AMAZON- READ THIS!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Ric Winter\XXXOOOXXX

LOL! So, I bought these for my violin, had them put on, and WOW!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Beautiful! My violin teacher loves them more than his Dominants. He had tried the Infeld blue, didn't care for them, but the RED has the gold E string that sounds so beautiful! I will never play on other strings. When I upgrade to a new violin when I get better, I will use these strings. WORD OF CAUTION:  Do NOT use cheap rosin on your bow with these strings!!!!!!!!!!! top_five_star_obscene.text: I'll give it 5 stars for now, I've yet to mess with it and don't like rating a product poorly over crap unrelated to the product itself. However, this was supposed to be a christmas gift for my girlfriend --it is that time of the year after all-- and someone had the grand idea of slapping a shipping label on the product box itself, no exterior amazon packaging whatsoever. Not much of a christmas surprise anymore considering I was in class when it arrived, and my girlfriend was home alone. Rushed home all excited to add another box to the dog cage of christmas crap, but that excitement quickly faded when I see that it's sitting on the kitchen table sans any kind of amazon packaging whatsoever. I mean, even if she wasn't home, you're really going to leave a box advertising some fancy electronic drum set on somebody's doorstep for all to see? I for one have never had a problem with anyone taking my crap off my doorstep, got a rather long driveway, but I know plenty of people who have, and this would be begging for it.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Christmas shoppers beware: Make sure you somehow specify that they ship it with an exterior box.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
If you're a musician, you know how cable shopping is. It's a crap shoot. Your choices are either a.) go down to the local music store and pay way too much or b.) order online and hope what you get isn't absolute crap. Over the years, you end up with a hodgepodge of cables that have lasted over a decade without so much as a cold solder, and cables that were such crap to begin with the only thing keeping you from using them as ties to hold up your tomato plants is that you're too lazy to garden and actually grow tomatoes.

These cables are the good kind. They're not super heavy duty, but thick enough. They're not the ones that seem like cell phone charger cable with XLR jacks (if you've ordered cheap cables more than a handful of times you know what I'm talking about). And the jacks work. No issues with any of them being misaligned, breaking, etc. I'm only a year or so in but so far so good. Also, they're plain jane which I like. No colored shrink wrap, overly fancy XLR connectors, obnoxious branding, or anything of the sort.
top_five_star_obscene.title: After a year they continue to function perfectly.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I've played the same Fender USA strat since 2011, and I've played it a lot. It's the first thing I would grab if my house was on fire. With that said, I didn't really want anything to replace it, but I wanted something smaller and easier to play in the bedroom and other places that make a smaller form a little more bearable (makes it so much easier to get into those port-o-potties).

Anyway, it IS much easier to play in more cramped spaces, BUT, holy crap is this thing fun to play. And it plays GREAT. The feel of it is awesome. The only downsides I have, which aren't enough to care about for me for this guitar, are the neck width (a little small for my hands, but I have big hands), and maybe the dead, bland feel of the wood in this guitar. It's still worth the money, but there's a way that good wood sounds when a guitar vibrates, and this once definitely has zero of that. But that isn't going to affect playability or how it sounds to a degree. And for the money, it's just fine. In fact that's maybe not even fair to gripe about at all with a guitar this cheap.

ALL new guitars need a proper setup. It also takes a few hours of playing to get it broken in a little bit. These headphones are old school CooooooL retro style which is what i like about them. VERY, VERY good external sound isolation, not sound cancelation! You can't hear shit with these things on which means you have to lift the no-lead side ear cup to converse with anyone. But thats fine. i don't want to talk much any way while doing music.

Very comfortable, especially if you have large ears or sensitive ears that get sore from direct pressure contact against them, like mine. I HATE things pushing against my ears and bending the shit out of them! Your ear sits completely inside the cups so you can wear them for hours no worries.

I play bass guitar using these headies and they have REALLY good bottom end audio frequency ranges and top ends are very solid also. The input jack has a neat wind on wind off set-up, with the 3.5 mini jack inside the main jack for ipods or tablets, or what ever. The Single sided lead is way better than those left and right Y shaped leads that get in the f.....g way for everything and anything!

Downsides? The only one i could think of was the curly spring jack lead, while VERY stretchy, it's too short for walking ALL around a room playing guitar, but its perfect for MOST uses because it doesn't tangle around your feet trying to trip you over at only 18-24 inches long unstretched. top_five_star_obscene.text: Every guitar is different no matter who makes it, every top has its own grain, and the wood makes the sound in any guitar. All kinds of things affect the sound of a guitar including how many coats of finish laquer are sprayed on it, too many coats will kill the tone.if you play 10 of these you will find that 2 stand out above the other 8 and that 4 will sound like utter crap, and the other 4 will be mediocre at best. This is just the nature of acoustic instruments, they all have their own personality and none are going to be exactly the same. Set up also affects them in many ways. If you set it up with low action , you will nkt be able to play with as much volume and therefore you will not get as much tone out of it. If4 you change the saddle and string nut to bone you will get more tone than with the cheap plastic. If you pick a guitar with a tight grain in the top, you are more likely to get a better sounding guitar th7an one with6 wider grains. If you use phosphor bronze strings like D'Addario's , you will get a crisper more responsive tone. In other words with a little education about what makes a guitar sound good you can pick a good one from any manufacturer who uses real solid spruce tops and some descent qaulity strings. Now, with all that said, I personally recomend playing a few of them and picking the best one out of the bunch. I did that at my local music store and
top_five_star_obscene.title: if you play 10 of these you will find that 2 stand out above the other 8 and that 4 will sound like utter crap

top_five_star_obscene.text:
For the price, these puppies rock! After trying to mix down using a pair of PC desktop speakers for years (and years), I finally realized why my mixes sounded like crap in my car (and everywhere else for that matter).
Granted, at first I thought they sounded like crap. However, this was due to listening to desktop speakers are designed to color the sound. These boys tell it like it is. If your mixes sound like crap, they'll tell you. If your mixes are sweet, they'll let you know.
If you're a seasoned mastering engineer, these are probably toys to you. However, if you're the average joe who with an average joe home studio, then these above-average joes are what you're looking for. And yes, you will find the sweet spot once they're placed just right.
I also like the blue power light; gives a nice blue ambiance in a dark studio.

The original nut was crap, and now this thing sounds better with $5 d'addarios on it than it did before with elixirs! Some of you probably don't like elixirs, but given my tone preferences, that is pretty damn impressive to me. The original nut was just dulling the crap out of the sound of the strings and I knew it needed to be replaced as soon as I got the guitar. And I can't tell you how grateful I am for the fact that these were drop in replacements as I do not have guitar tools or nut files and have never filed a nut before. I didn't have get ripped off by a guitar douche for something I could do myself YAY! One look at this nut compared to the original let me know it was almost a match but far superior. Just so happy with this for the money, the nut and the bridge pins alone are worth it. The saddle will definitely have to be filed unless you like slide a whole lot, it's way too tall, but the length should be right for most cheap import acoustics (the ones that need it the most) at least. The saddle is the right length for my Alvarez and my Fender Sonoran acoustic, and the compensation for intonation on it seems right. In the pictures I provided, the detached nut is the original. top_five_star_obscene.text: I purchased this cable to be able to get a balanced audio connection between a television and a pair of studio monitors I had that were just sitting around collecting dust.  The monitors have unbalanced RCA inputs, balanced TRS inputs, and balanced XLR inputs.  The television in question has HDMI inputs (one of which is an ARC connection...  however, ARC will only work to output audio to a device with the capability to accept ARC audio, which essentially makes it an ARC device), an optical or toslink output, and a 3.5mm audio output (the 3.5mm connection is more widely known as a headphone connection, however, it can be used for much more than a set of headphones), and that is it.  There are no other connections for audio on the back panel (or anywhere else for that matter) of the television.  This has become relatively common place among television manufacturers these days, as analog devices have long since begun to take a back seat to their much more prevalent digital counterparts.  And it is just kind-of bass-ackwards and largely unnecessary for manufacturers to go out of their way including analog audio connections on their televisions when the vast majority of consumers will never use them, let alone look at them or even know what they are called or for. top_five_star_obscene.text:
As cheap as these strings were, I am pleasantly surprised with the results achieved.
I picked up an old, cheap electric guitar that needed some work. It had been dropped on it's head.
After repairing and playing it, it still sounded like crap so I had these strings, they were cheap and I put them on. Only took about 5 minutes to restring and was well worth the effort.
Okay, the guitar (and I am too embarrassed to give the brand) isn't my Gibson Les Paul or my Fender American Strat, but damn, it sounds good now. If I didn't have so many guitars, I might keep this one but I will probably sell it to make room for another.
These were well worth the few dollars I spent on them. Honestly, I won't restring my Fender with them, I'll use the Fender 250 bullets. But these are great for about any electric. With guitars, crappy strings kill it. Great strings can make a bad guitar okay, not great.
I will continue buying and using these on my lower end guitars but will spend the few extra bucks on my high end guitars.
